coredata.dc:description: We developed a rapid and sensitive method for detecting tartrazine (Tar), a common food colorant, using zinc-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-doped CQDs). Synthesized from biowaste pigeon pod shells and zinc acetate, the Zn-doped CQDs exhibited a high quantum yield (47.63%) and were characterized by photo-stability, non-toxicity, and water solubility. The inner filter effect (IFE) allows selective attenuation of Zn-doped CQD fluorescence by Tar, enabling precise detection. The approach obtains a low detection limit of 20.35 ng/mL and a quantification limit of 61.69 ng/mL within a linear range of 0–100 ng/mL under optimal conditions when employing the Box-Behnken statistical design. The approach demonstrates excellent reproducibility, successfully detecting Tar in real food samples with recovery rates ranging from 89% to 102.2%. This high accuracy confirms the sensor's reliability and effectiveness in practical applications. This cost-effective and rapid technique presents a promising solution for monitoring Tar levels in food products.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We developed a rapid and sensitive method for detecting tartrazine (Tar), a common food colorant, using zinc-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-doped CQDs). Synthesized from biowaste pigeon pod shells and zinc acetate, the Zn-doped CQDs exhibited a high quantum yield (47.63%) and were characterized by photo-stability, non-toxicity, and water solubility. The inner filter effect (IFE) allows selective attenuation of Zn-doped CQD fluorescence by Tar, enabling precise detection. The approach obtains a low detection limit of 20.35 ng/mL and a quantification limit of 61.69 ng/mL within a linear range of 0–100 ng/mL under optimal conditions when employing the Box-Behnken statistical design. The approach demonstrates excellent reproducibility, successfully detecting Tar in real food samples with recovery rates ranging from 89% to 102.2%. This high accuracy confirms the sensor's reliability and effectiveness in practical applications. This cost-effective and rapid technique presents a promising solution for monitoring Tar levels in food products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food coloring enhances the appeal and flavor of various dishes. The market for natural food colors is also experiencing substantial growth, projected to expand significantly in the coming years (Wu, Pu, Huang, & Sun, 2020). However, the majority of naturally occurring food colors are difficult to extract, unstable, and sensitive to light, heat, and other chemicals (Ou et al., 2018). In contrast, synthetic food colors, widely used in the food processing industry, offer advantages such as low cost and resistance to fading. However, excessive consumption of artificial food colors may pose health risks (Yao et al., 2018). Therefore, it is important to closely regulate the kind and quantity of synthetic food colors used in food.

A frequently used synthetic food color that is a member of the azo dye family is tartrazine (Tar) (Tanaka & Toxicology, 2006), popularly known as Lemon Yellow (M. Wang, Zhao, & Chemical, 2015). The Tar has been demonstrated to have strong mutagenic and carcinogenic effects when used in excess (Haridevamuthu et al., 2024). The use of Tar in any food is prohibited in China, Malaysia, and many other nations (W. Zhang et al., 2009). It has a strict level of usage in some foods and is accepted as an addition in several nations. The maximum permitted dosage of Tar in these foods is 300 mg/kg in Canada, where it is only used in the production of frozen drinks, jams, beverages, sweets, cakes, syrups, and preserves (Tanaka & Toxicology, 2006). The Joint Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA, 2019) of the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) and the World Health Organisation (WHO) determined that 7.5 mg/kg of body weight is the permissible daily intake of Tar for people (Tanaka & Toxicology, 2006). However, concerns remain regarding the excessive addition of tar to food products. Therefore, it is essential to develop a simple, rapid, sensitive, and accurate method for detecting tar in foods to safeguard consumer health.

Right now, capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Ryvolová, Táborský, Vrábel, Krásenský, & Preisler, 2007), electrochemical method (Deng, Li, Li, & Huang, 2016; Karim-Nezhad et al., 2017), high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Tsai, Kuo, Shih, & analysis, 2015), HPLC-ultra violet (HPLC-UV) (Dixit, Khanna, & Das, 2010), high-performance ion chromatography (HPIC) (Q.-c. Chen, Mou, Hou, Riviello, & Ni, 1998) and enzyme-linked immune-sorbent assay (ELISA) (Lei et al., 2013), been suggested for the detection of Tar. However, the vast majority of these techniques are costly, time-consuming, need complex sample processing, and are difficult to use. As a result, an increasing number of scientists are focused on developing affordable, rapid, sensitive, and efficient technologies for the detection of tar. Fluorescence technology, in particular, has gained widespread use in chemical and biological analysis due to its cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, accuracy, ease of operation, and quick response times (Lv et al., 2018; C. Zhang et al., 2021).

Predominantly, CQDs are good for simple functionalisation and may be further doped with heteroatoms to increase their detection sensitivity after being doped with phosphorus (P) (Gong et al., 2017), sulphur (S) (C. Qu et al., 2019), nitrogen (N) (Y. Liu et al., 2019), chlorine (Cl) (N. Wang et al., 2018) and boron (B) (Jia et al., 2019), etc. Doped CQDs have been widely utilized up to this point in the detection of numerous compounds, such as nitrite (Sun et al., 2021), and azithromycin (Guo et al., 2021) and so on.

Both metallic and non-metallic dopants are capable of altering the fluorescence by altering the bandgap and introducing new energy levels (Mathew, Varghese, KR, & Pinheiro, 2023). With sp2 hybridization in the core and sp3 hybridization in the shell, it changes the surface structure and electron distribution of CQDs (Esrafili, Sharifi, Dinparast, & Modelling, 2017; L. Lin, Luo, Tsai, Wang, & Chen, 2018; Tejwan et al., 2021). By lowering the energy difference between the carbon orbital and the nonbonding (n) orbital, non-metallic dopants boost the quantum yield (Majnis, Adnan, Yeap, & Julkapli, 2024). In contrast, during carbonization or dehydration, metallic dopants alter the band structure by chelating to the functional groups of the precursors (Arul & Sethuraman, 2019). The increase in toxicity with different metal atoms is one concern with metal atom doping (Akram et al., 2023; Ghosh, Das, Das, Banerji, & Das, 2022). Therefore, the doped metal atom must not attribute toxicity to the doped CQDs and must be ecologically acceptable (Chelladurai et al., 2024). Zinc is a crucial component in many biological processes and is thought to be a very low-hazardous metal. Therefore, it is preferable to use zinc as a dopant to improve the characteristics of CQDs (Tammina et al., 2019).

To the best of our knowledge, for the first time, a Zn-doped CQDs-based fluorescent nano-sensor was created in the work to specifically and accurately measure Tar. The method employs an efficient one-step hydrothermal process to synthesize Zn-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs), using natural biowaste (pigeon pod shells) and zinc acetate. The raw materials are affordable and readily available, further highlighting the cost-effectiveness of the approach (Zhong et al., 2022). Our suggested fluorescent nano-sensor offers the advantages of simplicity, good selectivity, low cost, wide linear range, and high sensitivity, opening up a new way to monitor Tar in real food samples.

Pigeon Pod Shells was purchased from Local Market, Shirpur, India. Tar, Zinc Acetate Chemical reagents of analytical grade were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Quinine Sulphate, NaCl, CaCl2, FeCl3, KMnO4, CuSO4, MgSO4, Glucose, lactose, Sunset Yellow, Sudan, Methylene Blue, Amaranth, Methyl Red, 7.4 pH Buffer Tablets were procured from Loba Chemie, Chemicals. Pvt, Ltd Mumbai, India. Orange Juice, Soft Drinks, and Candy were purchased from Local Market, in Shirpur, India. A 12 kDa dialysis bag was purchased from Himedia Laboratories, Mumbai. The water utilized in this project was ultrapure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the size and morphology of CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs, images taken with a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) on a JEM-2011TEM (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV were used. The CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs optical properties were investigated by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra using a JASCO- FP-2800 International CO. Ltd, Japan and UV spectrophotometer 1700/1800 Shimadzu, Japan, respectively. The structure and surface chemical composition of Zn-doped CQDs were investigated by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) using an ESCALAB 250 x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and a Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany), respectively. The FT-IR spectra of Zn-doped CQDs were recorded at a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the wavenumber range of 500–4000 cm−1. Raman spectroscopy analysis was done by Horiba Labra HR evolution with excitation lasers. The crystalline nature of the Zn-doped CQDs was analyzed by using XRD from PXRD Rigaku Smart Lab SE X-ray powder diffractometer REMI Laboratory Instruments, Mumbai.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Pigeon pod shell pretreatment: The Cajanus cajan (pigeon pod) was taken from the local area (Shirpur), Maharashtra. After being sorted, the pod shells were wiped with doubled distilled water (DDW) to eliminate the foreign matter. Thereafter the cleaned pod shells underwent a regulated temperature at 500 °C for 1 h for the drying process in a hot air oven. Furthermore, perfectly dried shells were cut into tiny fragments, and then ground to convert into a fine powder state (Mindivan & Göktaş, 2023).

For CQDs preparation, pretreated 1 g of finely grounded pigeon pod shell powder was soaked in 30 mL of DDW and executed for stirring at 1000 rpm for 1 h. After the end of the stirring period, the obtained dispersion was filtered to confiscate other unsolvable materials (Zhao et al., 2023). Finally, a Teflon line hydrothermal is filled with the previously blended dispersion. Then, the hydrothermal was placed into a hot air oven at 160 °C for 7 h. The autoclave was left unattended until it achieved 30 °C after the hydrothermal process was accomplished (Deme et al., 2023). Subsequently, to segregate the nanosized carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the obtained dark brown solution was centrifuged for 40 min at 13,000 rpm. The unreacted carbon particles were then removed by subjecting the centrifuged CQD solution to dialysis for 48 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 12 kDa. Afterwards, the dialysis solution containing the CQDs was collected and subjected to lyophilization using a laboratory freeze dryer (Y. Zhang et al., 2023).

For the preparation of Zn-doped CQDs, a similar procedure to that of the CQDs was followed. However, during the filtration of the pigeon pod shell powder dispersion, 10 mL of DDW containing 200 mg of zinc acetate was added. The subsequent steps remained consistent with the previously described procedure (Singh & Singh, 2023).

The Box-Behnken statistical experimental design (BBD), which was used to optimize the Zn Doped-CQDs preparation conditions, served as the foundation for the RSM. Three distinct levels, labelled −1, 0, and +1, were created for the three independent components A, B, and C. The following factors were chosen: the time was A (h), the temperature was B (°C), and the zinc acetate concentration was C (mg) as depicted in Table S1. The responsibility for determining whether the condition was excellent or bad was the relative fluorescence intensity (Zeyadi et al., 2024).

For the detection of Tar, 2 mL of the above Zn-doped CQDs solution (pH 7.4) was added to a 4 mL quartz cell, and 1000 μL Tar solutions with varied concentrations (0–100 ng/mL) were added to the system. The temperature of the system was maintained at 4 °C. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at Ex of 350 nm after 10 min of incubation. The selectivity and specificity of the generated sensing probe are essential for the reliable use of the Zn-doped CQDs in the detection of Tar (Mousa, Abdelrahman, Fahmy, Ebrahim, & Moustafa, 2023). A variety of metal ions and biomolecules were tested against the sensitivity and selectivity of Zn-doped CQDs. A sample of the stock solutions of nontarget samples (Na+, Ca+2, Fe+3, K+, Cu+2, Mg+2, Glucose, Lactose, Sunset Yellow, Sudan, Methylene Blue, Amaranth, Methyl Red) was produced in DDW for selectivity research. To assess specificity, a concentration of 100 ng/mL was used for all salts and organic substances. A total of 2000 μL of the Zn-doped CQD solution (pH 7.4) was combined with 1000 μL of each compound, and the reaction mixture was thoroughly mixed (Cao et al., 2024). The Zn-doped CQDs solution was mixed into other salts and substances at the appropriate concentrations. All of the sample's fluorescence spectra were acquired at 350 nm with a 5.0 nm slit width after 10 min, at both excitation and emission wavelengths.

Food samples, including orange juice, soft drinks, and candies from the local market, were selected for analysis. The orange juice was subjected to centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was then collected and diluted with DDW. The resulting sample solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm microfilter. The recovered filtrate was stored in a freezer at 4 °C for further investigation (R. Liu, Li, et al., 2022). The soft drink was filtered using a microfilter (0.45 μm) after being subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 30 min to remove CO2. For later usage, the recovered filtrate was kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C. For the candy sample, 10 mL of warm water was added to 5 g of precisely measured crushed candy. A microfilter (0.45 μm) was used to filter the sample solution for candies. For subsequent investigation, the recovered filtrate was kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C (R. Liu, Li, et al., 2022).

Utilizing the three independent BBD factors of various synthetic conditions, including time (B: 4, 7, and 10 h), temperature (A: 140, 160, and 180 °C), and zinc acetate concentration (C: 150, 200, and 250 mg) at three levels, the combined effect on the emission intensities of the Zn doped-CQDs solutions was examined (Table S2). The response was the intensity of the emission at 350 nm. In the studies, several combinations of the three variables were used. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to further assess the model's dependability and accuracy. The p-values for the interaction models, as shown in Table S3, were below 0.05, demonstrating that the model could successfully predict the emission intensity of the Zn-doped CQDs solutions. However, the quadratic regression model's determination R-squared (R2) was 0.9800, indicating that the model could be utilized to explain 98.00% of the variability.

The high adjusted R-squared (R2 a) of 9199, which was close to R2 (Yetilmezsoy, Demirel, & Vanderbei, 2009), indicated that the model was significant and had an outstanding match to the experimental data. The model's high accuracy and dependability were further enhanced by its low coefficient of variation (CV) (0.7614%) (Maran, Manikandan, Thirugnanasambandham, Nivetha, & Dinesh, 2013).

Temperature 160 °C, duration = 7 h, and zinc acetate concentration = 200 mg were the best results the Design Expert program could produce. Three times the altered settings were used in replication tests of the Zn doped-CQDs preparation (Table S4). The model's predictive intensity was 4543, compared to the three replication trials' average intensity of 4512. The difference between the actual and predicted intensity was estimated to be 0.68 %. Such a negligible inaccuracy demonstrated the model's accuracy in forecasting intensity. Notably, the Zn-doped CQDs (Tabel S2, std batch-13) was made under these modified ideal circumstances. As a result, in the studies that followed, (std batch-13) was chosen as the optimized batch for synthesis of Zn-doped CQDs.

Design Expert 8.0.6.1 (equation (1)) produced a second-order polynomial equation by the RSM model. The coefficients for A, B, and C were −23.25, 13.25, and 3.45 in the equation, respectively, showing that B > C > A might have an impact on intensity. In other words, out of the three parameters, the effect of temperature was the most impacted (Su, Deng, Ye, He, & Cui, 2020). A Fig. S1 displayed the contour plot and 3D response surface between the three variables and the response. More elliptical trend patterns on the contour plot represented a higher interaction between the two variables (Wei et al., 2020). As a result, the degree of the intensity of the interaction between A, B, and C was found in the following order: AC > BC > AB.
equation (1) Y = 4543–23.25 A + 13.25 B + 3.45 C + 1.75 AB + 36.25 AC + 9.75 BC – 200.12 A2 – 176.12 B2 – 200.13 C2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment's optimized batch of Zn-doped CQDs was characterised by the following factors: time (7 h), temperature (160 °C), and zinc acetate concentration (200 mg).

For fluorescence analysis, the greenly generated naked CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs were exposed to a range of wavelengths in a UV cabinet Fig. S2. As a result, in visible light, the naked CQDs solution appeared bright yellow Fig. S2 (a). When exposed to UV light with excitation wavelengths of 254 nm and 365 nm, bare CQD solutions showed bright green fluorescence and blue fluorescence, respectively Fig. S2 (b, c).

Under visible light, the introduction of Zn-doped CQDs shows a dark yellow colour Fig. S2 (d). Additionally, it fluoresced strongly blue at 365 nm and looked green under a maximum 254 nm illumination Fig. S2 (e, f) respectively. This demonstrates how the fluorophore adheres to the CQD surface, increasing the fluorescence of Zn-doped CQDs.

The bared CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs were created, which is shown by the UV–visible spectrophotometry analysis. The bare CQD has a maximum absorption peak wavelength of 267 nm, according to the UV Spectra shown in Fig. 1 (a) , and it exhibits an n-π∗ transition that indicates the presence of the C=O, C-O, and -OH group in the bare CQDs (Deka & Chowdhury, 2017). As a result, it verifies the efficacy of creating CQDs from their natural precursor. Fig. 1 (b) showed the absorption peak at 276 nm as depicted in the UV absorption spectrum of the manufactured Zn-doped CQDs. The C=O containing n-π∗ transitions are said to be driven by the functionality of either -COOH or -OH groups (Chang, Kung, Venkatesana, Teng, & Lee, 2024). As shown in Fig. 1 (c), the wide spectra of Zn-doped CQDs change towards a longer wavelength (276 nm) by the mechanism of the bathochromic effect, as a result of the addition of zinc acetate, confirming the presence of Zn doping (P. Das et al., 2018). Subsequently, this study is supported by FTIR analysis.

The FTIR analysis is used to validate the presence of the various functional groups in the substance. The bare CQDs displayed an absorption band with a broad peak at 3256 cm−1, indicating hydroxyl (-OH) stretching vibration, and strong peaks at 2926 cm−1, 1770 cm−1, 1574 cm−1, 1379 cm−1, 1068 cm−1 indicating C-H stretching vibration, the carboxylic acid (C=O) stretching vibration, C=C, C-H (Bending) vibrations, and epoxy (C-O) stretching vibrations groups, respectively as per Fig. 2 (a) . The provided information verified that green synthesized CQDs had carboxylic functionality on their outside (Kayani et al., 2024). Fig. 2 (b) elucidates strong absorption band peaks visible in the FTIR spectra of Zn-doped CQDs at wavelengths of 2355 cm−1, 1558 cm−1, and 936 cm−1, respectively, signifying the stretching of O=C=O, the stretching of C=C, and the bending of C=C. The Zn-O/Zn-OH bands significant peak at about 776 cm−1 shows that oxygen and the metal formed complexes (P. Das et al., 2018). The intensities of the distinctive zinc-doped CQDs peaks at 1770 cm−1 (C=O) and 3256 cm−1 (-OH) were found to be lower in the FTIR spectra of the zinc-doped CQDs (Zn-doped CQDs) compared to control CQDs (undoped). This polar functional group's presence suggests that the Zn-CQDs as produced have great water solubility. We can attest to the success of Zn doping (T. Das, Mojumder, Saha, & Pal, 2024).

The zeta potential study verified that bared CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs have a surface charge. The stability of the solution is determined in large part by the zeta potential. According to states, the zeta potential of green synthesis CQDs is −6.57 mV (Fig. S3 (a)). Importantly, the negative zeta value supported the oxygen-based surface functionality seen in CQDs, including epoxy, carboxyl, and hydroxyl (Pimpang, Sumang, & Choopun, 2018). The charge potential of the produced Zn-doped CQDs is −17.33 mV (Fig. S3 (b)), indicating that the produced Zn-modified CQDs include the functional groups 'Zn' with hydroxyl and carboxyl can be attributed to the existence of the above functional groups and demonstrates that the Zn-doped CQDs aqueous solution is stabilized electrostatically. Zeta potential, as a consequence, ensured that Zn-doped CQDs would be more stable than bare CQDs (P. Das et al., 2018).

To investigate the effects of pH on the FL intensity of Zn-doped CQDs, the pH-dependent emission was assessed. The Zn-doped CQDs were created at several different pH levels. At a 350 nm excitation wavelength, the photoluminescence intensity of a solution containing synthesized Zn-doped CQDs was observed. Furthermore, a 1 M solution of NaOH and 1 M HCl was used to change the pH of the Zn-doped CQDs solution to 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, and 14 (Fig. S4). Since pH 5 displays the highest fluorescence spectra compared to other alkaline and acidic pH solutions (3.0, 10.0), Briefly stated, the protonation and deprotonation of surface-contained functional groups caused a significant impact on the electronic transition processes inside defect states, which in turn caused PL intensity to fluctuate, (Fig. S5) (Yang et al., 2019).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to characterize the morphology and size of the Zn-doped CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 (a) (b) , Zn-doped CQDs are shown to be even and nearly spherical with sizes smaller than 10 nm. It reveals that prepared Zn-doped CQDs were uniform in size ranging from 2 to 9 nm in diameter and average particle size 5 ± 0.5 nm as depicted in Fig. 3 (c) (Lu et al.). SAED patterns of Zn-doped CQDs as per Fig. 3 (d) showed diffused rings indicating the amorphous phase of the nanoparticles (Pal, Mohiyuddin, & Packirisamy, 2018).

Scanning electron microscopy represented the surface morphology of the Zn-doped CQDs as shown by Fig. S6 (a). In essence, Zn-doped CQDs showed a variety of sizes and irregular shapes. Additionally, it demonstrates the consistent distribution of Zn-doped CQDs (Kang et al., 2020).

EDAX spectroscopy confirms the elemental structure of synthesized Zn-doped CQDs. Fig. S6 (b) showed the Zn-doped CQDs EDAX spectrum, which ensured the presence of "C," "O," and "Zn" at roughly 47.25 wt percent, 50.26 wt percent, and 2.49 wt percent, respectively. Zn is present in CQDs, which is an interesting sign of doping in the structure of CQDs. Zn-doped CQD's elemental composition, therefore, proves that they were created for doped CQDs.

The XPS technique was used as a characterizing tool to explore the surface chemical characteristics, material compositions, and bonding inside the Zn-doped CQDs nanoparticles. Fig. 4 . (a) show the successfully formed zinc-doped carbon quantum dots as well as the bonding states of the dots. The core level binding energy peaks for C 1s (285 eV), Zn 2p (1023 eV, 1047 eV), and O1s (532 eV) are visible in spectra. All of these results support the Zn-doped nanoparticles' successful production (P. Das et al., 2018). Additionally, the creation of a chemical bond as well as a fractional electron transfer from metallic zinc atoms to the inorganic oxygen atoms must be taken into consideration. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 4 (b)), three distinct carbon components appear at 284.2 eV, 285.8 eV, and 287.1 eV respectively, which could be attributed to C=O, C–C, and O=C-O bonding (D. Qu et al., 2014). The O1s spectra peak at 531.8 eV and 533.2 eV (Fig. 4 (c)) can be assigned to C–O and Zn-O binding respectively (Jiang et al., 2015). The Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 4 (d)) presented two predominant features at 1043.5 eV and 1020.5 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively (J.-H. Lin et al., 2012).

The Zn-doped CQDs XRD spectrum shows two broad diffraction peaks (2 thetas = 24օ-31օ, 36օ-48օ), which point to the presence of extremely disordered carbon that was likely put together randomly in Fig. S7 (Hara et al., 2004). Due to the tiny crystalline domains in the nanoparticles, the XRD peaks of the samples are wide (Shamsipur, Rajabi, & C, 2014). The drop in ordered sp2 layers that depend on high defective carbon structures on the Zn-doped CQDs might be the cause of the shift of the as-synthesized Zn-doped CQDs relative to the normal peak (002) at 26.50° (J. Peng et al., 2012)

Raman spectroscopy can be used to calculate the sample's sample crystallinity for the graphite phase. In general, the strength of the D-peak (approximately 1325 cm−1) indicates the in-plane stretching vibration of the C atom sp2 hybridization, while the intensity of the G-peak (about 1500 cm−1) represents the defect of the C atom lattice. The ratio of I(D)/I(G)is 0.987. The ratio of I(D)/I(G) is less than 1, which indicates that CQDs have some degree of graphite phase structure, as illustrated in Fig. S8 (Zhou et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL spectra of bare CQDs and Zn-doped CQD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S9 showed both the synthesized bare CQDs and the Zn-doped CQDs were stimulated at 350 nm excitation wavelength at the same concentration. Due to the fluorophore atom Zn being on the outside of the CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of Zn-Doped CQDs is increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL spectra of bared CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs at a different excitation wavelength
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra were taken at various wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 400 nm to evaluate the enhanced photonic capabilities of fabricated CQDs. The emission spectra of bare CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs were then recorded at different wavelengths. The emission maximum moves towards a longer wavelength, or redshift, at 350 nm excitation, and the emission intensity progressively rises with increasing excitation value from 310 to 350 nm (Mehta et al., 2015). As a result, Fig. 5 (a) depicts bare CQDs exhibiting excellent fluorescence intensity at 360 nm (excitation wavelength), and their emission peak at 426 nm coincides with the n-π∗ transition of the carbonyl or carboxyl group connected to surface functionality and Fig. 5 (b) showed Zn-doped CQDs which exhibited outstanding fluorescence intensity at 350 nm (excitation wavelength) and a peak in emission at 425 nm, which agrees with surface functionality-related n-π∗ transition of the carbonyl or carboxyl group and incorporation of fluorophores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excitation and emission of CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Redshift is produced by the excitation wavelength-dependent emission in hydrothermally synthesized bared CQDs. It was intriguingly discovered that emissive traps and differences in particle size dispersion reveal the excitation-dependent PL intensity of CQDs. The manufactured CQDs exhibit the maximum fluorescence intensity at a 360 nm excitation wavelength and a 426 nm emission wavelength Fig. 5 (c). The synthesized Zn-doped CQDs emit light at a wavelength of 425 nm and exhibit the greatest fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm as depicted in Fig. 5 (d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Zn-doped CQDs to tar
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence capacity of the nano-sensors and each component was evaluated using a spectrofluorometric method. A greenly treated Zn-doped CQDs solution emits at 425 nm when stimulated at 350 nm excitation wavelength. The amount of fluorescence increased noticeably when zinc acetate was added to the CQDs in concentration. This may be due to the Zn-doped CQDs' obvious complexation. It is demonstrated in how Zn-doped CQDs are used for Tar monitoring. In conclusion, the final Zn-doped CQDs showed higher fluorescence intensity when compared to produced CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs, which is useful for sensing applications.

The initial fluorescence intensity was reduced when Tar dosages of 0 ng/mL to 100 ng/mL were added, (Fig. 6 (a) ). The "Turn Off" in this context refers to the dimming of the nanoprobe fluorescence intensity. Importantly, the predicted nanoprobe offers a broad concentration range as a response, from 0 ng/mL to 100 ng/mL. The calibration curve yields the values Y = 0.0396x + 0.9787 and R2 = 0.9911 and demonstrates a good linear connection between fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) and Tar content (Fig. 6 (b)). The synthesized nano-sized sensor's LOD and LOQ were also established to be 20.35 ng/mL and 61.69 ng/mL, respectively. Overall, the proposed nano-sensor revealed Tar's excellent responsiveness. It could be due to the nanosized Zn-doped CQD's large surface area, porous structure, strong binding affinity, number of electron pairs containing functional groups, etc.

Fluorescence quenching was seen with the addition of Tar in Zn-doped CQDs, which may have developed as a result of static, dynamic quenching, the IFE method, or the FRET process. According to the literature, the inner filter effect causes fluorescence quenching when the absorbance of the analyte overlaps with the emission and excitation spectra of the probe (Shuai Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2018). Fig. 7 , may infer from the fact that the decrease in fluorescence intensity of Zn-doped CQDs caused by the addition of Tar may have been caused by the inner filter effect (Kayani et al., 2024) since the emission spectra of Zn-doped CQDs in our probe coincided with the absorbance spectra of Tar.

The selectivity and specificity of the generated sensing probe are essential for the reliable use of the Zn-doped CQDs in the detection of Tar. A variety of metal ions and biomolecules were tested against the sensitivity and selectivity of Zn-doped CQDs as shown in Fig. 8 . A sample of the stock solutions of nontarget samples (Glucose, creatinine, glycine, urea, ZnCl2, NaCl, NH4Cl, KCl, Sunset Yellow, Sudan, Methyl Blue, Amaranth, Methyl Red) was produced in distilled water for selectivity research. However, the maximum fluorescence intensity quenching was observed by Tar than other nontarget samples. From this, we can conclude that the synthesized Zn-doped CQDs probe was only developed for the very specific and selective detection of Tar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of tar in actual real sample

ce:section.ce:para: This research presents a novel approach for estimating Tar using doped carbon quantum dots. For the first time, a novel fluorescent nanosensor based on Zn-doped CQDs was developed for the ultra-sensitive detection of Tar. This method effectively establishes a natural precursor for synthesizing both CQDs and Zn-doped CQDs. By employing zinc acetate as the zinc source and utilizing green biowaste, specifically pigeon pod shells, as the carbon source, we synthesized Zn-doped CQDs through a straightforward one-pot hydrothermal process. The synthesized Zn-doped CQDs exhibit high emission fluorescence and good water solubility. Compared to bare CQDs (20.19% quantum yield), Zn-doped CQDs demonstrated a significantly higher quantum yield of 47.63%. The synthesized Zn-doped CQDs were employed as fluorescent nanosensors for the monitoring of Tar, with a wide linear range (0–100 ng/mL), low detection limit (20.35 ng/mL), and limit of quantification (61.69 ng/mL).

The Tar quenched the fluorescence of the Zn-doped CQDs through an inner filter effect (IFE), as the absorption spectrum of Tar coincided with the emission spectra of the Zn-doped CQDs nanoprobe. In light of this, a "TURN-OFF" sensor platform was established. The developed fluorescent nanosensor effectively detected Tar in real food samples, yielding satisfactory results. With relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 1.47%, the recovery rates ranged from 89% to 102.23%. Finally, the measurement of Tar in food samples was performed with high accuracy and good precision. The proposed fluorescent nanosensor offers several advantages, including easy fabrication, label-free operation, and no need for complex pretreatment. It operates under mild detection conditions, is environmentally friendly, cost-effective, highly sensitive, and selective, offering simple operation with accurate results. It is anticipated that Tar detection will have many potential applications in areas like food management and food safety.

Khan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.
